pages.raw_text:
Plans for a concrete bridge at
Monroe street. prepared in City
Engineer Ralstons office, were fin
ally adopted by the city council
last night, and it is believed that
work on the structure may actually
be begun in a comparatively short
time. The plans call for rather
plain but neat structure, with a 30
foot roadway and nine foot side.
walks on either side.


Three arches, the main one 240
feet across and the smaller ones at
either end 1870 feet across, accord
ing to the plans, will be under the
bridge, which can be built for
about 1896, 900, according to Rails
toms estimate. Councilman Dalke
started something that was hard to
stop, though, sort Of prediction
thorn, in the estimation of some OF
the others present, when he moved
that the plans be adopted and that
the board of public works be in
structed to go right ahead with the
work, which has been hanging fire
for many months.


At a previous meeting. when the
plans were up for consideration,
Councilman Lambert moved that
the board of public works be in
structed to go ahead and advertise
for bids, consequently Dalke
amended his motion to the effect
that the board either advertise for
bids or go ahead with the day labor
plan. The day labor plan was op-
posed by Lambert, who maintained
that the city would be put to
greater expense than if they should
have the bridge built by some re-
putable firm, as a plant, which
would be left on hand as mere
junk would have to be purchased.
Schiller then called attention to
the fact that there was only 100000
in the bridge fund at the present
time and that after the election, so
near at hand, perhaps a sufficient
amount of money for the construc-
ton of the bridge would not be
voted. but that he piers could be
